that we've been through our testing process and we've found audiences at work, we've found ads at work, the next phase is to shift them into our prospecting campaigns. for example, we've paused it and we'll come back to it in a later date to try and reignite it. But right now, we have a campaign here that's performing. For the time being, that's fine, because it's the highest performer as well. You might be tempted to say, I want more traffic and more budget to hear. But here's the thing about CBO campaign budget optimization and why I like it so much is Facebook is making these decisions for me in terms of where to spend the money based on where it believes the highest and more consistent return can come in. So all of these ad sets are being optimized for purchase. This is our prospecting campaign. That's the event we want. And what we're doing right now is to build stability before we look at scaling. So one of the things that I see people make a mistake on is going into the prospecting stage, seeing good performance and just absolutely wanted to increase those budgets 20% day, maybe even duplicate and do 100% or bigger without testing for stability. And that's the process we've gone through here, because at the same time as running these ads, we're actually optimizing the landing page. So if you just bear in mind, you know, if you are increasing your budget, your your I would recommend doing that when you're confident that your funnel is as high performing as possible. So for example, we want to get our page load speeds faster for this particular set of landing pages. And we want to tweak and improve conversion rates. So at this stage, although we're getting good good performance on budgets, and we are going to start increasing the budgets gradually, we're actually optimizing the funnel to maximize conversion rate, then we'll come back to being a lot more aggressive on the budget. So this is how we build stable results is by going through the testing process, we're introducing ads and audiences that have a higher chance of performing when we put bigger spends and more consistent spend across them. But secondly, we're looking for stable results before we start scaling. So if I bring my audiences into this stage, I would let them run for three to five days before I consider increasing budgets, because I want that stability. Then at that stage,

## [06:08 - 09:11]

I would start increasing my bids, looking at different types of scaling strategies, as long as my optimizations are in place. Broad works for us, especially in ecom, it's harder in niches like lead gen digital products, etc. Because certain digital products can be quite divisive. And also, you want to attract the right people which Facebook doesn't necessarily do as good a job on. So for example, if I'm doing a broad audience and I'm offering my training course, for example, as an ad, it will most likely get a lot of negative comments, because people don't want to be pushed things that they don't want to buy, especially when they think it might be a scam or, you know, they've been burnt in the past, whereas for ecom, broad audiences can work really well. So I would definitely make it part of your testing mix. In our case, it's been through phase one, phase two. And now it's in phase three, it's actually proving to be our most successful ad set so far. Now, one of the other things I want to point out at this stage is, although at the testing stage, I wasn't too overly concerned about how my campaigns were structured. At this stage, once we're at the prospecting stage and kind of prior to optimization and scaling, I do want to introduce a bit more structure around my naming conventions and what ad sets are contained within my campaigns. So generally, what I do is I group one to 3% lookalikes in a separate campaign to four to 7% and eight to 10%. The reason is what I found when you're running CBO campaigns, and I'm predominantly on CBO because I believe in the way it works. And I love the way it takes out a lot of the day to day decisions from me, and it optimizes as well as I could as well, especially when you've got the inputs right. And there's a separate section on understanding CBO. But essentially, what we've also found is the more similar the audiences, the better the stabilization and performance. So for example, at the very basic level, you can see with split out interests, broad and lookalikes, that gives us more consistency. And we've tested running lookalikes and interests in the same campaign, as separate ad sets, for

## [12:15 - 15:15]

example, and kind of broad and lookalikes together, it creates a level of instability because Facebook at the campaign level trying to figure out where to put its money into which ad sets, but the ad sets are very different. So for that reason, we at the basic level split out lookalikes, interests, and broad. And that gives us a lot more stability as well. So that's just something to bear in mind when you are structuring your kind of main accounts. And these are when I when I talk about main accounts, I'm talking about non test accounts. So these are now, we've got proven ads and audiences and now we're looking to prospect and scale up. ## [15:16 - 16:22]

change or most of the changes you introduce, especially new campaigns and especially new ad sets and new ads, it causes the system to go back into learning phase. Now, learning phase has more of an impact if you don't have data at the ad set level than when you do. So if you have an existing ad set, it's been running, it's got hundreds or thousands of dollars of spend behind it and you introduce a new ad. Generally, it shouldn't cause instability because you've got enough data at the ad set level for it to stabilize. But if you don't have enough data and you're doing small spends, it can create a problem. So just bear that in mind. And what we do there, and we'll go through the optimization stage, is we'll try and give it a chance and maybe pause it, give it another chance, pause it until we can get that stability back as well. So that's really about the prospecting stage. Use it with the graduation testing process, test your audiences, test your ads, get it into the stage, and then we go into the optimization stage before we look at scaling.
